learning_outcomes.description: Identify basic combinational and sequential circuit blocks used in digital system design along with their VHDL descriptions
Analyse a problem description to be solved with digital hardware and breakdown the problem into sub-systems with well-defined functionality
Formulate an end-to-end digital hardware design in VHDL with individual sub-systems defined at suitable abstraction, and integrating the sub-systems to meet the required specifications

Design digital systems via synthesizable VHDL descriptions, Datapath and Controller approach, and model-based design approaches and verify the functionality through simulation, synthesis, and implementation steps on FPGA hardware
Develop testbenches in VHDL to test digital systems for required functionality
Design digital systems optimised for speed/area/power in field programmable gate array (FPGA) based platforms
Design and develop speed/area optimised digital hardware architectures for real-time implementation of digital signal processing algorithms
Present technical details of a design via technical reports and oral presentations
Relate the technology and methods used in your designs to recent findings in literature and suggest improvements

assessments.due_date:
Task 0 11/08/2026 4:00 pm

Task 1 25/08/2026 4:00 pm

Task 2 8/09/2026 4:00 pm

Task 3 22/09/2026 4:00 pm

Task 4 13/10/2026 4:00 pm

Task 5 30/10/2026 4:00 pm

For Task 0, there is no demonstration. For Tasks 1-4, students will be required to demonstrate their design during the scheduled lab sessions in weeks 5, 7, 9, and 11, respectively. For Task 5, demonstrations will be in exam week 1 (scheduling information will be provided during the semester). For tasks 1-5, marks will be capped based on the demonstration, which will be outlined within the task description. For tasks 1-5, if a student misses the originally allocated demo, then a second demo attempt will be provided based on documented evidence (such as a medical condition). For tasks 1-5, if both demo attempts are missed, then 0 marks will be awarded for that particular task, irrespective of the Blackboard submission.
assessments.category: Computer Code, Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Practical/ Demonstration
assessments.task_description:
Laboratory work consists of weekly practicals leading to a sequence of 6 assessment tasks with their contribution towards final course mark as indicated below:

Task 0: 6% (Blackboard submission)

Task 1: 6% (Blackboard submission+demonstration)

Task 2: 6% (Blackboard submission+demonstration)

Task 3: 6% (Blackboard submission+demonstration)

Task 4: 6% (Blackboard submission+demonstration)

Task 5: 10% (Blackboard submission+demonstration)

For each task, students are required to complete a Blackboard submission. For tasks 1-5, there will be an identity verified oral assessment as well, where students will need to demonstrate their design to teaching staff and answer questions. For tasks 1-5, marks will be capped based on the demonstration, which will be outlined within the task description. For tasks 1-5, if a student misses the originally allocated demo, then a second demo attempt will be provided based on documented evidence (such as a medical condition). For tasks 1-5, if both demo attempts are missed, then 0 marks will be awarded for that particular task, irrespective of the Blackboard submission.

The Use of AI tools

The lab tasks have been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or machine translation (MT) technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission via Blackboard. There will also be practical demonstrations during the scheduled lab sessions in weeks 5,7,9,11, and exam week.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
This final examination tests all the material covered in this course during the whole semester. The emphasis is on testing your design problem solving skills.

The Use of AI tools

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
Lecture

One 2-hour lecture per week. Refer to the detailed teaching outline on Blackboard for more information about public holidays and refer to student timetable for any updates on lecture location. Lectures will be recorded and made available on Blackboard, however there is no guarantee that the recordings will always work. In-class quizzes (as formative assessments) will be used in lectures to reinforce learning and to provide on-going feedback. These quizzes will not contribute towards the final grade but will train students for the final exam.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08, L09

learning_activities.topic:
Applied Class

One 1-hour applied class per week, problem solving and applied examples are discussed.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08, L09
learning_activities.topic_description: Applied Class One 1-hour applied class per week, problem solving and applied examples are discussed.

learning_activities.topic:
Practicals

One 2-hour lab session per week. Practicals sessions will be conducted in-person at 47-401. You must sign up for one prac session out of the sessions available. It is highly recommended that you do the required background work and complete the tasks as much as you can before attending the practical classes. Labs are based on electronic design and automation (EDA) tools AMD/Xilinx Vivado and Matlab. The software is not Mac OS compatible natively and Mac users will need to rely on virtual containers (the performance can be slow) or lab PCs to complete the tasks. Sufficient lab time is provided to complete the lab tasks and additional lab access could be provided on request (based on the lab availability).

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05, L06, L07, L08, L09
